Libraries import

In [25]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Loaded the data.csv file for analysis and showed the first 5 rows.

Ploted the graph of the product categories.

df['Product line'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')

Checked the frequency of the sales.

In [34]:
df['Sales'].plot(kind='hist')
plt.show()

Heat map to show the correleation between the data.

In [35]:
sns.heatmap(df.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True)
plt.show()
In [44]:
df.groupby('Product line')['Sales'].sum().plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Total Sales by Product Line")
plt.show()
In [38]:
df['Gender'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Gender Distribution")
plt.show()
In [39]:
df['Payment'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Payment Method Distribution")    
plt.show()

Coclusion: Fashion & accessories have the most sales followed by food & beverages. The sales histogram is right skewed which means that the low cost products have been sold more than the high cost products. Generated more revenue with the food and beverages. Most of the products have been sold to female than males. And Ewallet and Cash were mostly equally used for transactions dominating Credit cards.

Machine Learning

Predict total sales amount based on input features which means If I know the quantity and unit price, can I predict how much the sale will be?

In [47]:
X = df[['Quantity', 'Tax 5%', 'Unit price']]
y = df['Sales']
In [48]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
In [49]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[49]:
LinearRegression()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
fit_intercept  True
copy_X  True
tol  1e-06
n_jobs  None
positive  False
In [50]:
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
In [51]:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error

mean_absolute_error(y_test, predictions)
Out[51]:
9.283240842705709e-14
In [52]:
plt.figure()

plt.plot(y_test.values, label='Actual')
plt.plot(predictions, label='Predicted')

plt.legend()
plt.title("Actual vs Predicted Sales")
plt.show()
In [53]:
plt.figure()

plt.scatter(y_test, predictions)
plt.xlabel("Actual")
plt.ylabel("Predicted")
plt.title("Actual vs Predicted")

plt.show()

Not a perfect model. Trying another model

In [63]:
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df, drop_first=True)
In [64]:
X = df_encoded.drop('Rating', axis=1)
y = df_encoded['Rating']
In [65]:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor

model = RandomForestRegressor()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

Didnt go well as well

Lets do time series forecasting

In [106]:
daily_sales = df.groupby('Date')['Sales'].sum()
In [107]:
df.isnull().sum()
Out[107]:

In [108]:
daily_sales = daily_sales.sort_index()
In [110]:
daily_sales.index
Out[110]:
Index(['1/1/2019', '1/10/2019', '1/11/2019', '1/12/2019', '1/13/2019',

In [112]:
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])
In [113]:
df = df.set_index('Date')
In [114]:
daily_sales = df['Sales'].resample('D').sum()
In [115]:
daily_sales.plot()
plt.show()
In [116]:
daily_sales.head()
Out[116]:
Date
2019-01-01    4745.1810
2019-01-02    1945.5030
2019-01-03    2078.1285
2019-01-04    1623.6885
2019-01-05    3536.6835
Freq: D, Name: Sales, dtype: float64
In [117]:
daily_sales.plot()
plt.title("Daily Sales Over Time")
plt.show()

The Graph is random bcs of small amount of data however I will do the forecasting anyway...

In [118]:
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
In [119]:
model = ARIMA(daily_sales, order=(5,1,0))
model_fit = model.fit()
In [120]:
forecast = model_fit.forecast(steps=10)
In [121]:
plt.figure()

plt.plot(daily_sales, label='Actual')
plt.plot(forecast, label='Forecast', color='red')

plt.legend()
plt.title("Sales Forecast")
plt.show()

The forecast converges toward an average value, indicating that in the absence of strong patterns, the model predicts a stable mean level of sales

Conclusion

The time series analysis shows that daily sales exhibit high variability with no clear trend or seasonal pattern. As a result, the ARIMA model produces a relatively stable forecast centered around the mean sales value. This suggests that sales are largely driven by random fluctuations rather than predictable temporal patterns.
